title: Specific Performance & Recovery of Possession
statute_text: Specific Relief Act 1877, Section 9: a person dispossessed of immovable property without their consent, otherwise than in due course of law, may file a suit to recover possession, and the only defense available to the dispossessor is to show a better title - questions of legal title generally cannot otherwise be raised in this summary remedy. This section is a fast, simplified remedy specifically for wrongful/forcible dispossession, distinct from a full ownership/title suit. The Act also governs specific performance of contracts (compelling a party to actually perform their contractual obligation, rather than just pay damages) and injunctions (preventive relief).
elements_required: For Section 9 possession claims: claimant was in possession of immovable property
Claimant was dispossessed without their consent and otherwise than through due legal process (i.e., wrongfully/forcibly, not via a court order or lawful process)
Suit filed within the limitation period (6 months from dispossession per the Limitation Act 1908)
For specific performance: a valid, enforceable contract exists and damages alone would not be an adequate remedy
common_defenses: For Section 9: dispossessor shows better title to the property (the one defense the Act itself allows in this summary proceeding)
Dispossession did not actually occur without consent, or occurred through due legal process (e.g., pursuant to a valid court order)
Suit filed beyond the 6-month limitation window - possession suits under Section 9 are time-sensitive
For specific performance: damages would be an adequate remedy, or the contract itself is not one courts will specifically enforce (e.g., personal service contracts)
procedural_notes: Federal law - applies uniformly across Pakistan including Punjab
Section 9 is a summary, fast-track remedy - it deliberately does NOT allow deep litigation of ownership/title questions, only the fact of wrongful dispossession
Strict 6-month limitation period applies to Section 9 possession suits specifically - much shorter than ordinary property suits
A person with a stronger underlying legal claim (e.g., actual title) may still need to pursue a separate, full title suit if the 6-month window for the summary remedy has passed
example_scenario: A tenant is forcibly locked out of a shop by the landlord without any court order or legal notice. Rather than waiting to pursue a full ownership/tenancy dispute, the tenant can file a Section 9 suit for recovery of possession within 6 months, focusing narrowly on the fact of wrongful dispossession rather than the underlying merits of the tenancy dispute itself.
sources: Limitation Act 1908 First Schedule (cross-referencing Section 9's 6-month period)
archive.org full text of the Limitation Act 1908 (cross-reference)
review_status: NOT reviewed by a licensed lawyer - verify current statute text against pakistancode.gov.pk before production use; specific performance provisions in particular need deeper verification beyond what's summarized here
